([/kænˈtɑːtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); Italian: [[kanˈtaːta]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); literally "sung", past participle feminine singular of the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") verb *cantare*, "to sing") is a [vocal](/wiki/Vocal_music "Vocal music") [composition](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") with [instrumental](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") [accompaniment](/wiki/Accompaniment "Accompaniment"), typically in several [movements](/wiki/Movement_(music) "Movement (music)") and often involving a [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Vocal composition with an instrumental accompaniment

For other uses, see [Cantata (disambiguation)](/wiki/Cantata_(disambiguation) "Cantata (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Soloquartet_and_strings.jpg/330px-Soloquartet_and_strings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soloquartet_and_strings.jpg)

Soloquartet and strings

A **cantata** ([/kænˈtɑːtə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); Italian: [[kanˈtaːta]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); literally "sung", past participle feminine singular of the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") verb *cantare*, "to sing") is a [vocal](/wiki/Vocal_music "Vocal music") [composition](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") with [instrumental](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") [accompaniment](/wiki/Accompaniment "Accompaniment"), typically in several [movements](/wiki/Movement_(music) "Movement (music)") and often involving a [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir").

The meaning of the term changed over time, from the simple single-voice [madrigal](/wiki/Madrigal_(music) "Madrigal (music)") of the early 17th century, to the multi-voice "cantata da camera" and the "cantata da chiesa" of the later part of that century, from the more substantial dramatic forms of the 18th century to the usually sacred-texted 19th-century cantata, which was effectively a type of short [oratorio](/wiki/Oratorio "Oratorio").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Cantatas for use in the liturgy of church services are called [church cantata](/wiki/Church_cantata "Church cantata") or sacred cantatas; other cantatas can be indicated as secular cantatas. Several cantatas were, and still are, written for special occasions, such as [Christmas cantatas](/wiki/Christmas_cantata "Christmas cantata"). [Christoph Graupner](/wiki/Christoph_Graupner "Christoph Graupner"), [Georg Philipp Telemann](/wiki/Georg_Philipp_Telemann "Georg Philipp Telemann") and [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") composed cycles of [church cantatas](/wiki/Church_cantata_(Bach) "Church cantata (Bach)") for the occasions of the liturgical year.

## Historical context

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Historical context")]

The term originated in the early 17th century, simultaneously with [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") and [oratorio](/wiki/Oratorio "Oratorio").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[2]](#cite_note-2) Prior to that, all "[cultured](/wiki/High_culture "High culture")" music was vocal. With the rise of [instrumental music](/wiki/Instrumental "Instrumental"), the term appeared, while instrumental art became sufficiently developed to be embodied in [sonatas](/wiki/Sonata "Sonata"). From the beginning of the 17th century until late in the 18th, the cantata for one or two solo voices with accompaniment of [basso continuo](/wiki/Figured_bass#Basso_continuo "Figured bass") (and perhaps a few solo instruments) was a principal form of Italian vocal [chamber music](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

A cantata consisted first of a declamatory narrative or scene in [recitative](/wiki/Recitative "Recitative"), held together by a primitive [aria](/wiki/Aria "Aria") repeated at intervals. Fine examples may be found in the church music of [Giacomo Carissimi](/wiki/Giacomo_Carissimi "Giacomo Carissimi"); and the English vocal solos of [Henry Purcell](/wiki/Henry_Purcell "Henry Purcell") (such as *Mad Tom* and *Mad Bess*) show the utmost that can be made of this archaic form. With the rise of the [da capo aria](/wiki/Da_capo_aria "Da capo aria"), the cantata became a group of two or three arias joined by recitative. [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel")'s numerous Italian duets and trios are examples on a rather large scale. His Latin [motet](/wiki/Motet "Motet") *Silete Venti*, for soprano solo, shows the use of this form in church music.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Differences from other musical forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Differences from other musical forms")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[*Wo ist doch mein Freund geblieben*, BuxWV 111](/wiki/File:Wo_ist_doch.ogg "File:Wo ist doch.ogg")

A sacred cantata by [Dieterich Buxtehude](/wiki/Dieterich_Buxtehude "Dieterich Buxtehude")

---

[10. Chorale: *Jesus bleibet meine Freude*](/wiki/File:Bach,_BWV_147,_10._Jesus_bleibet_meine_Freude.ogg "File:Bach, BWV 147, 10. Jesus bleibet meine Freude.ogg")

From the cantata [*Herz und Mund und Tat und Leben*, BWV 147](/wiki/Herz_und_Mund_und_Tat_und_Leben,_BWV_147 "Herz und Mund und Tat und Leben, BWV 147") by Johann Sebastian Bach (Orchestra Gli Armonici)

---

*Problems playing these files? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

The Italian solo cantata tended, when on a large scale, to become indistinguishable from a scene in an [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), in the same way the church cantata, solo or choral, is indistinguishable from a small oratorio or portion of an oratorio. This is equally evident whether one examines the church cantatas of Bach, of which nearly 200 are extant (see [List of Bach cantatas](/wiki/List_of_Bach_cantatas "List of Bach cantatas")) or the *[Chandos Anthems](/wiki/Handel_at_Cannons#Chandos_Anthems "Handel at Cannons")* of Handel. In Johann Sebastian Bach's case, some of the larger cantatas are actually called oratorios, and the *[Christmas Oratorio](/wiki/Christmas_Oratorio "Christmas Oratorio")* is a collection of six church cantatas actually intended for performance on six different days, though together they form as complete an artistic whole as any classical oratorio.

## Baroque

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Baroque")]

See also: [Church cantata](/wiki/Church_cantata "Church cantata") and [List of church cantatas by liturgical occasion](/wiki/List_of_church_cantatas_by_liturgical_occasion "List of church cantatas by liturgical occasion")

During the baroque era, the term "cantata" generally retained its original Italian usage to describe a secular vocal piece of extended length, often in different sections, and usually Italianate in style. At the same time, vocal pieces of similar scope, often with several singers, and various instruments, were in great demand for the services of the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") church. Such pieces were usually called *geistliche Konzerte* (singular: *geistliches Konzert*, meaning sacred concerto). Many of these pieces were simply called by their opening text. Such pieces for the [liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") or other occasions were not only composed by Bach but also by [Dieterich Buxtehude](/wiki/Dieterich_Buxtehude "Dieterich Buxtehude"), [Christoph Graupner](/wiki/Christoph_Graupner "Christoph Graupner"), [Gottfried Heinrich Stölzel](/wiki/Gottfried_Heinrich_St%C3%B6lzel "Gottfried Heinrich Stölzel") and [Georg Philipp Telemann](/wiki/Georg_Philipp_Telemann "Georg Philipp Telemann"), to name a few. The editors of the [Bach Gesellschaft](/wiki/Bach_Gesellschaft "Bach Gesellschaft") adopted "sacred cantata" as a convenient catchall for most of Bach's liturgical pieces. The term was then retroactively applied by [Philipp Spitta](/wiki/Philipp_Spitta "Philipp Spitta") to refer to comparable works by composers from [Heinrich Schütz](/wiki/Heinrich_Sch%C3%BCtz "Heinrich Schütz") onwards.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Many secular cantatas were composed for events in the nobility. They were so similar in form to the sacred ones that many of them were [parodied](/wiki/Parody#Music "Parody") (in parts or completely) to sacred cantatas, for example in Bach's *Christmas Oratorio*.

### Bach cantatas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bach cantatas")]

Main articles: [Bach cantata](/wiki/Bach_cantata "Bach cantata") and [List of Bach cantatas](/wiki/List_of_Bach_cantatas "List of Bach cantatas")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=). *(April 2018)* |

Johann Sebastian Bach, almost 200 of whose cantatas survive, is a notable contributor to the genre.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

His cantatas are usually written for a baroque orchestra consisting of a string section, an oboe section, and a [continuo](/wiki/Basso_continuo "Basso continuo") group. Timpani and brass were sometimes added on festive occasions such as Christmas or Easter. The vocal forces consisted of a four-part choir and soloists. Bach also wrote some cantatas for only one solo singer (ex. [BWV 51](/wiki/BWV_51 "BWV 51")).

A handful of his cantatas, such as *[Tritt auf die Glaubensbahn,](/wiki/Tritt_auf_die_Glaubensbahn,_BWV_152 "Tritt auf die Glaubensbahn, BWV 152")* [BWV 152](/wiki/Tritt_auf_die_Glaubensbahn,_BWV_152 "Tritt auf die Glaubensbahn, BWV 152") (1714), were written as [dialogue cantatas](/wiki/Bach_cantata "Bach cantata"). These cantatas represented interactions between two parties, mostly between Jesus, played by a bass, and the Soul, played by a soprano.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Graupner's cantatas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Graupner's cantatas")]

[Christoph Graupner](/wiki/Christoph_Graupner "Christoph Graupner") was Hofkapellmeister at the court of [Hesse-Darmstadt](/wiki/Hesse-Darmstadt "Hesse-Darmstadt") and provided over 1,400 cantatas during his nearly 50 years of employment there, making him the most significant contributor to the genre. While only a handful of Bach's cantatas contain accompanied chorales (the vocal parts are usually doubled by the instrumental parts), nearly all of Graupner's chorales feature elaborate ritornello sections.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This is possibly because Bach's Leipzig congregation was expected to sing along with them, but the Darmstadt court was not. Also, many of Graupner's cantatas exploit elaborate orchestral effects and use exotic instrumentation, such as [chalumeau](/wiki/Chalumeau "Chalumeau"), [flûte d'amour](/wiki/Fl%C3%BBte_d%27amour "Flûte d'amour"), [oboe d'amore](/wiki/Oboe_d%27amore "Oboe d'amore"), [viola d'amore](/wiki/Viola_d%27amore "Viola d'amore"), [trumpets](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), [horns](/wiki/Horn_(instrument) "Horn (instrument)") and [timpani](/wiki/Timpani "Timpani"). See: [List of cantatas by Christoph Graupner](/wiki/List_of_cantatas_by_Christoph_Graupner "List of cantatas by Christoph Graupner").

## Classical and romantic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Classical and romantic period")]

The term "cantata" came to be applied almost exclusively to choral works, as distinguished from solo vocal music. In early 19th-century cantatas, the chorus is the vehicle for music more lyric and songlike than in oratorio, not excluding the possibility of a brilliant climax in a [fugue](/wiki/Fugue "Fugue") as in [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven")'s [Der glorreiche Augenblick](/wiki/Der_glorreiche_Augenblick "Der glorreiche Augenblick"), [Carl Maria von Weber](/wiki/Carl_Maria_von_Weber "Carl Maria von Weber")'s *Jubel-Kantate*, and [Felix Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn "Felix Mendelssohn")'s *[Die erste Walpurgisnacht](/wiki/Die_erste_Walpurgisnacht "Die erste Walpurgisnacht")*. [Anton Bruckner](/wiki/Anton_Bruckner "Anton Bruckner") composed several [Name-day](/wiki/Name-day "Name-day") cantatas, a [Festive Cantata](/wiki/Festive_Cantata_(Bruckner) "Festive Cantata (Bruckner)") and two secular cantatas (*[Germanenzug](/wiki/Germanenzug "Germanenzug")* and [*Helgoland*](/wiki/Helgoland_(Bruckner) "Helgoland (Bruckner)")). Bruckner's [Psalm 146](/wiki/Psalm_146_(Bruckner) "Psalm 146 (Bruckner)") is also in cantata form. Mendelssohn's [Symphony Cantata](/wiki/Choral_symphony "Choral symphony"), the *[Lobgesang](/wiki/Lobgesang "Lobgesang")*, is a hybrid work, partly in the oratorio style. It is preceded by three symphonic movements, a device avowedly suggested by Beethoven's [Ninth Symphony](/wiki/Ninth_Symphony_(Beethoven) "Ninth Symphony (Beethoven)"); but the analogy is not accurate, as Beethoven's work is a symphony of which the fourth movement is a choral finale of essentially single design, whereas Mendelssohn's *Symphony Cantata* is a cantata with three symphonic preludes. The full lyric possibilities of a string of choral songs were realized by [Johannes Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms") in his *[Rinaldo](/wiki/Rinaldo_(cantata) "Rinaldo (cantata)")*, which, like the *Walpurgisnacht*—was set to a text by [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe"). Other cantatas, Beethoven's *Meeresstille*, works of Brahms and many notable small English choral works, such as cantatas of [John Henry Maunder](/wiki/John_Henry_Maunder "John Henry Maunder") and [John Stanley](/wiki/John_Stanley_(composer) "John Stanley (composer)"), find various ways to set poetry to choral music. The competition for the French [Prix de Rome](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome "Prix de Rome") requires that each candidate submit a cantata. [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz") failed in three attempts before finally winning in 1830 with *[Sardanapale](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome_Cantatas_(Berlioz) "Prix de Rome Cantatas (Berlioz)")*. While almost all of the Prix de Rome cantatas have long since been forgotten (along with their composers, for the most part), Debussy's prize-winning *L'enfant prodigue* (1884, following his unsuccessful *Le gladiateur* of 1883) is still performed occasionally today. Late in the century, [Gustav Mahler](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler") wrote his early *[Das klagende Lied](/wiki/Das_klagende_Lied "Das klagende Lied")* on his own words between 1878 and 1880, and [Samuel Coleridge-Taylor](/wiki/Samuel_Coleridge-Taylor "Samuel Coleridge-Taylor") created a successful [trilogy](/wiki/Trilogy "Trilogy") of cantatas, *[The Song of Hiawatha](/wiki/The_Song_of_Hiawatha_(Coleridge-Taylor) "The Song of Hiawatha (Coleridge-Taylor)")* between 1898 and 1900.

## Twentieth century and beyond

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantata&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Twentieth century and beyond")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article may **require [cleanup](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")** to meet Wikipedia's [quality standards](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style "Wikipedia:Manual of Style"). The specific problem is: **Recentism and WP:INDISCRIMINATE. See comment in wiki text for more information.** Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Cantata "Special:EditPage/Cantata") if you can. *(April 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Cantatas, both of the chamber variety and on a grand scale, were composed after 1900 as well.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Indeed, it would not be an exaggeration to claim that one of the most popular pieces of classical music of the 20th century to the layman's ears, is a cantata, namely *[Carmina Burana](/wiki/Carmina_Burana_(Orff) "Carmina Burana (Orff)")* (1935–1936) by the German composer [Carl Orff](/wiki/Carl_Orff "Carl Orff").

In the early part of the century, secular cantatas once again became prominent, while the 19th-century tradition of sacred cantatas also continued. [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") composed both kinds: "festival" cantatas such as *[Toward the Unknown Region](/wiki/Toward_the_Unknown_Region "Toward the Unknown Region")* (1907), *Five Mystical Songs* (1911), and *[Five Tudor Portraits](/wiki/Five_Tudor_Portraits "Five Tudor Portraits")* (1936), and sacred cantatas including *Sancta civitas* (1926), *Benedicite* (1930), *[Dona nobis pacem](/wiki/Dona_nobis_pacem_(Vaughan_Williams) "Dona nobis pacem (Vaughan Williams)")* (1936), and *Hodie* (1954). [Joseph Ryelandt](/wiki/Joseph_Ryelandt "Joseph Ryelandt") also composed secular and sacred cantatas, such as *Le chant de la pauvreté* Op. 92 in 1928 and *Veni creator* Op. 123 in 1938. [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók") composed the secular *[Cantata Profana](/wiki/Cantata_Profana "Cantata Profana")*, subtitled "The Nine Splendid Stags" and based on a Romanian folk tale, in 1930. Although it began as a song cycle (as reflected also by its title), [Arnold Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg")'s *[Gurre-Lieder](/wiki/Gurre-Lieder "Gurre-Lieder")* (1900–1903/1910–11) evolved into one of the century's largest secular cantatas. [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith") composed three works he designated as cantatas: *Die Serenaden*, Op. 35, for soprano, oboe, viola, and cello (1924), *Mahnung an die Jugend, sich der Musik zu befleissigen* (from the *Plöner Musiktage*, 1932), and *Ite angeli veloces* for alto and tenor, mixed chorus, and orchestra, with audience participation (1953–55). Of [Anton Webern](/wiki/Anton_Webern "Anton Webern")'s last three compositions, two are secular cantatas: Cantata No. 1, Op. 29 (1938–39), and Cantata No. 2, Op. 31 (1941–43), both setting texts by [Hildegard Jone](/wiki/Hildegard_Jone "Hildegard Jone"). Webern had begun sketching a Third Cantata by the time he was killed in 1945. [Ernst Krenek](/wiki/Ernst_Krenek "Ernst Krenek") also composed two examples: a "scenic cantata", *Die Zwingburg*, Op. 14 (1922), and a *Cantata for Wartime*, Op. 95, for women's voices and orchestra (1943). [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev") composed *Semero ikh* (1917–18; rev. 1933), and in 1939 premiered a cantata drawn from the film music for *[Alexander Nevsky](/wiki/Alexander_Nevsky_(Prokofiev) "Alexander Nevsky (Prokofiev)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He wrote two festival cantatas, the *[Cantata for the Twentieth Anniversary of the October Revolution](/wiki/Cantata_for_the_20th_Anniversary_of_the_October_Revolution_(Prokofiev) "Cantata for the 20th Anniversary of the October Revolution (Prokofiev)")*, Op. 74, and *Flourish, Mighty Homeland*, Op. 114, for the thirtieth anniversary of the same event

Patriotic cantatas celebrating anniversaries of events in the [Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") or extolling state leaders were frequently commissioned in the Soviet Union between 1930 and the middle of the century, though these occasional works were seldom among their composers' best. Examples include [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich")'s *Poem of the Motherland*, Op. 47 (1947) and *The Sun Shines over Our Motherland*, Op. 90 (1952), and three works by Prokofiev, *Zdravitsa!* [Hail to Stalin] (1939). [Dmitry Kabalevsky](/wiki/Dmitry_Kabalevsky "Dmitry Kabalevsky") also composed four such cantatas, *The Great Homeland*, Op. 35 (1941–42), *The Song of Morning, Spring and Peace*, Op. 57 (1957–58), *Leninists*, Op. 63 (1959), and *About Our Native Land*, Op. 82 (1965).

Patriotic cantatas were also created in China during the [civil war](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") and the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"). For example, the [Yellow River Cantata](/wiki/Yellow_River_Cantata "Yellow River Cantata") was composed in 1939.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1940, the Brazilian composer [Heitor Villa-Lobos](/wiki/Heitor_Villa-Lobos "Heitor Villa-Lobos") created a secular cantata titled *Mandu çarará*, based on an Indian legend collected by Barbosa Rodrigues. [Francis Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc") composed in 1943 *[Figure humaine](/wiki/Figure_humaine "Figure humaine")*, [FP](/wiki/FP_(Poulenc) "FP (Poulenc)") 120, a cantata for double mixed [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir") of 12 voices on poems by [Paul Éluard](/wiki/Paul_%C3%89luard "Paul Éluard"). [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky") composed a work titled simply *[Cantata](/wiki/Cantata_(Stravinsky) "Cantata (Stravinsky)")* in 1951–52, which used stanzas from the 15th-century "Lyke-wake Dirge" as a narrative frame for other anonymous English lyrics, and later designated *[A Sermon, a Narrative and a Prayer](/wiki/A_Sermon,_a_Narrative_and_a_Prayer "A Sermon, a Narrative and a Prayer")* (1961) as "a cantata for alto and tenor soli, speaker, chorus, and orchestra". [Luigi Nono](/wiki/Luigi_Nono_(composer) "Luigi Nono (composer)") wrote *[Il canto sospeso](/wiki/Il_canto_sospeso "Il canto sospeso")* in 1955–56. [Hans Werner Henze](/wiki/Hans_Werner_Henze "Hans Werner Henze") composed a *Cantata della fiaba estrema* and *Novae de infinito laudes* (both in 1963), as well as a number of other works that might be regarded as cantatas, such as *Kammermusik* (1958, rev. 1963), *Muzen Siziliens* (1966), and *[El Cimarrón](/wiki/El_Cimarr%C3%B3n_(musical_work) "El Cimarrón (musical work)")* (1969–70). *[Momente](/wiki/Momente "Momente")* (1962–64/1969), one of the most important works of [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen"), is often described as a cantata. [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten") composed at least six works he designated as cantatas: *[The Company of Heaven](/wiki/The_Company_of_Heaven "The Company of Heaven")* (1937), *[Rejoice in the Lamb](/wiki/Rejoice_in_the_Lamb "Rejoice in the Lamb")*, Op. 30 (1943), *[Saint Nicolas](/wiki/Saint_Nicolas_(Britten) "Saint Nicolas (Britten)")*, Op. 42 (1949), the *[Cantata academica](/wiki/Cantata_academica "Cantata academica")*, Op. 62 (1959), the *Cantata Misericordium*, Op. 69 (1963), and *[Phaedra](/wiki/Phaedra_(cantata) "Phaedra (cantata)")*, Op. 93 (1975). [Alberto Ginastera](/wiki/Alberto_Ginastera "Alberto Ginastera") also composed three works in this form: the *Cantata para América Mágica*, Op. 27 (1960), *Bomarzo*, Op. 32 (1964), and *Milena*, Op. 37 (1971), and [Gottfried von Einem](/wiki/Gottfried_von_Einem "Gottfried von Einem") composed in 1973 *An die Nachgeborenen* based on diverse texts, the title taken from a poem of [Bertolt Brecht](/wiki/Bertolt_Brecht "Bertolt Brecht"). [Mikis Theodorakis](/wiki/Mikis_Theodorakis "Mikis Theodorakis") composed the cantatas *According to the Sadducees* and *Canto Olympico*. [Herbert Blendinger](/wiki/Herbert_Blendinger "Herbert Blendinger")'s *Media in vita* was premiered in 1980, his *Mich ruft zuweilen eine Stille* (Sometimes a silence calls me) in (1992), and *Allein den Betern kann es noch gelingen* (It can only be achieved by those who pray) in 1995. [Iván Erőd](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Er%C5%91d "Iván Erőd") wrote in 1988/89) *Vox Lucis* (Voice of the Light), Op. 56. [Ivan Moody](/wiki/Ivan_Moody_(composer) "Ivan Moody (composer)") wrote in 1995 *Revelation*.

Cantatas were also composed by [Mark Alburger](/wiki/Mark_Alburger "Mark Alburger"), [Erik Bergman](/wiki/Erik_Bergman "Erik Bergman"), [Dave Brubeck](/wiki/Dave_Brubeck "Dave Brubeck"), [Carlos Chávez](/wiki/Carlos_Ch%C3%A1vez "Carlos Chávez"), [Osvald Chlubna](/wiki/Osvald_Chlubna "Osvald Chlubna"), [Peter Maxwell Davies](/wiki/Peter_Maxwell_Davies "Peter Maxwell Davies"), [Norman Dello Joio](/wiki/Norman_Dello_Joio "Norman Dello Joio"), [Lukas Foss](/wiki/Lukas_Foss "Lukas Foss"), [Roy Harris](/wiki/Roy_Harris "Roy Harris"), [Arthur Honegger](/wiki/Arthur_Honegger "Arthur Honegger"), [Alan Hovhaness](/wiki/Alan_Hovhaness "Alan Hovhaness"), [Dmitry Kabalevsky](/wiki/Dmitry_Kabalevsky "Dmitry Kabalevsky"), [Libby Larsen](/wiki/Libby_Larsen "Libby Larsen"), [Jón Leifs](/wiki/J%C3%B3n_Leifs "Jón Leifs"), [Peter Mennin](/wiki/Peter_Mennin "Peter Mennin"), [Dimitri Nicolau](/wiki/Dimitri_Nicolau "Dimitri Nicolau"), [Krzysztof Penderecki](/wiki/Krzysztof_Penderecki "Krzysztof Penderecki"), [Allan Pettersson](/wiki/Allan_Pettersson "Allan Pettersson"), [Daniel Pinkham](/wiki/Daniel_Pinkham "Daniel Pinkham"), [Earl Robinson](/wiki/Earl_Robinson "Earl Robinson"), [Ned Rorem](/wiki/Ned_Rorem "Ned Rorem"), [William Schuman](/wiki/William_Schuman "William Schuman") (*[A Free Song](/wiki/A_Free_Song "A Free Song")*), [Roger Sessions](/wiki/Roger_Sessions "Roger Sessions"), [Siegfried Strohbach](/wiki/Siegfried_Strohbach "Siegfried Strohbach"), [Michael Tippett](/wiki/Michael_Tippett "Michael Tippett"), [Kurt Weill](/wiki/Kurt_Weill "Kurt Weill"), [Jörg Widmann](/wiki/J%C3%B6rg_Widmann "Jörg Widmann") (*[Friedenskantate](/wiki/Friedenskantate_(Widmann) "Friedenskantate (Widmann)")*, *Cantata in tempore belli*) and [Jan Ryant Dřízal](/w/index.php?title=Jan_Ryant_D%C5%99%C3%ADzal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jan Ryant Dřízal (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jan_Ryant_D%C5%99%C3%ADzal "cs:Jan Ryant Dřízal")] (*Christmas Cantata*).
